agent1_raw: The benchmark is not apples-to-apples. Four things changed simultaneously with the model: the prediction endpoint (24h sepsis vs 12h vasopressor onset), the lab timestamp convention (charted vs specimen time), the cohort unit (admissions vs merged episodes), and the random split grain (encounter-level creating patient leakage). A model comparison that changes evaluation conditions alongside model architecture cannot support a promotion decision. The external Hospital B results and the clinically reasonable feature handling suggest a harmonized rerun would be worthwhile.
agent2_raw: Reading across paragraphs, the LSTM's published performance numbers come from a 24-hour severe-sepsis endpoint, while the transformer is optimized for 12-hour vasopressor escalation. These are not the same target. The timestamp handling also differs by paragraph: the LSTM study used EHR-charted timestamps, while the refreshed registry uses specimen collection time when the LIS provides it. The cohort construction rule changed too — individual admissions versus merged transfer episodes. And encounter-level random splitting in a five-year warehouse with recurring ICU patients means some patients appear in both train and holdout. Each of these is a confound. The external validation at Hospital B is the cleanest comparison available, but it does not resolve the benchmark's internal problems.
agent3_raw: The memo is well-structured but the benchmark has four embedded design flaws. Endpoint: the target shifted from a 24-hour screening task to a 12-hour specific escalation task. Timestamps: the LSTM protocol used charted lab times; the transformer pipeline uses specimen collection time. Cohort: the LSTM was built on admissions from the legacy ADT feed; the transformer uses merged cross-transfer episodes. Split: encounter-level random splitting lets recurring ICU patients contribute to both training and holdout. These flaws prevent attribution of the AUROC and recall differences to the model architecture. A clean rerun is needed.
synthesis_raw: Three agents agree on empirical_test_agreed. The four design mismatches are consistently identified across all agents. None of the agents dismisses the red herrings (lactate/vasopressor features before prediction time, 1126-episode external cohort with prespecified power) as invalidating. All agents agree a harmonized rerun should be run before promotion. ETD: harmonized rerun with shared endpoint, timestamp policy, cohort construction rule, and patient-level chronological split.
all_issues_raised: Prediction target changed from 24-hour severe-sepsis to 12-hour vasopressor onset
Lab timestamping changed from EHR-charted to specimen collection time
Encounter-level random split causes patient-level leakage in multi-admission ICU dataset
Cohort unit changed from individual ADT admissions to merged cross-transfer episodes

empirical_test.condition: Rebuild shared cohort with one endpoint, one timestamp policy, one cohort construction rule, patient-level chronological split
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: AUROC advantage falls below 0.01 and recall improvement disappears under harmonized conditions
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer maintains at least 0.02 AUROC lead and 5+ recall points at same alert rate on both hospitals
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Some gain persists internally but not externally, or AUROC holds but recall advantage disappears
